briefing.opening: Hello everyone. I will now begin the climate briefing for April 1, 2026.

sections.title: ⚠️ Deepening Climate Risks and Asset Impacts
sections.content: According to news collected today, extreme weather events are acting as 'climate risks' to assets, such as declining individual home values, with half of voters experiencing this and growing demands for the introduction of carbon taxes [2, 5]. Internationally, direct impacts of climate change are appearing in various regions, including ecological damage to Canada's marine economy, winter warming in the Western U.S., and extreme convective weather in Southern China [154, 155, 160]. Specifically, an Irish advisory council has warned that climate change will cause widespread damage across Ireland in 2025, and in Australia, ecological threats are severe, with wildlife damage from tropical cyclones and the potential extinction of the superb fairy-wren being raised [164, 165, 166, 167]. Furthermore, the case of the major power outage in Spain suggests the need for pace adjustment during the renewable energy transition, and the Middle East conflict is causing raw material supply difficulties for the renewable energy industry, thus increasing concerns about energy security and stability [104, 117]. As such, climate change is evolving into complex risks across a wide range of areas including the economy, ecosystems, and social infrastructure, and discussions have even begun on its impact on educational outcomes [163].

sections.title: 🌱 Accelerating Domestic Efforts Towards a Carbon-Neutral Society
sections.content: Domestically, various efforts to transition to a carbon-neutral society are accelerating. The amendment to the 'Framework Act on Carbon Neutrality' passed the Cabinet meeting, strengthening the policy foundation [28], and technological development is also active, with a Kookmin University professor team being selected for a climate change adaptation ecosystem restoration and carbon sink enhancement project [1]. The participation of local governments, businesses, and civil society is also notable. Seongnam City and Yecheon County are conducting carbon neutrality education for the elderly [8, 35, 79, 89], Sokcho City has introduced 'Carbon Neutral Schools' [17, 80], and Dongdaemun-gu is operating 'Carbon Neutral Playgrounds,' focusing on education and awareness expansion [33, 58, 86]. Several companies, including Harim, Hana Financial Group, and Shinhan Financial Group, are participating in spreading a culture of carbon neutrality practice through Arbor Day tree planting, clothing donations, and the 'Serious about Energy' campaign [11, 16, 29, 39, 51, 54, 62, 66, 71, 72, 73, 76, 83, 84, 91, 93, 95, 98, 102], and industrial cooperation is also strengthening, with Seoul City Gas, Airrane, and GRES signing an MOU for carbon-neutral energy business cooperation in the Seoul metropolitan area [21, 23, 30, 32, 41, 52, 57, 60, 87].

sections.title: 🌱 Accelerating Renewable Energy Transition and Industry Trends
sections.content: The transition to renewable energy is accelerating further to address climate change and strengthen energy security. The Ministry of Climate is accelerating policy shifts by equating 'energy security' with 'renewable energy,' and plans to expand renewable energy support and ESS (Energy Storage System) construction by allocating a supplementary budget of 524.5 billion won [82, 109, 111, 137]. Globally, renewable energy generation is approaching 50% of the total, with solar power generation, in particular, showing rapid growth, increasing by 511 GW in just one year [114]. Domestically, large corporations are actively participating, with Hyundai Motor Group investing 9 trillion won to create an AI, hydrogen, and renewable energy complex in Saemangeum, and IT companies like Kakao and Naver signing PPA (Power Purchase Agreement) contracts to expand renewable energy [105, 108]. At the local government level, Chuncheon City has declared securing an additional 500 MW of renewable energy by 2035 and is pursuing a transition to an 'energy self-sufficient city' [110, 120, 126, 129, 131, 135, 136], and Donghae City is promoting renewable energy supply projects for residential homes, expanding regional renewable energy distribution [106, 118, 139, 142]. In the industry, Hanwha Q CELLS has launched 'New Homes,' an integrated renewable energy solution targeting the U.S. residential market [119, 121, 125, 130, 140, 143, 149], and Kolon Global is embarking on new business model development and market expansion, initiating the nation's first private wind V.PPA (Virtual Power Purchase Agreement) electricity trading [127, 133, 138, 146].

sections.title: 🌍 Domestic and International Climate Change Policies and Awareness Expansion
sections.content: Policy discussions and efforts to raise awareness about climate change are continuously ongoing both domestically and internationally. Domestically, Yeosu City is participating in international climate action by holding a review meeting to prepare for the 'UN Climate Change Convention Climate Week' event [6, 9], and KoSIF sent questionnaires to political parties regarding ESG, climate, and renewable energy policies ahead of local elections, urging policy attention [128]. Internationally, policy attempts to hold polluters accountable and share costs are active, with the U.S. state of Vermont defending a law that charges fossil fuel companies for climate change costs, and the Maine Legislature passing a bill to study the costs incurred by climate change [150, 157, 162]. Furthermore, research and reports continue to highlight the scientific aspects and actual impacts of climate change, such as a study showing that climate change alters day length [151] and a report on its influence on Mississippi's weather [153]. Moreover, a broader understanding of climate change's impact on society, culture, and individual lives is spreading, exemplified by the emergence of 'eco-chaplains' as new spiritual caregivers for those grappling with climate change issues [156], and discussions on the definition and importance of climate change taking place in Azerbaijan [161].

briefing.closing: Today's briefing demonstrates that despite the deepening climate risks, concrete actions and policy efforts for carbon neutrality and renewable energy transition are actively underway both domestically and internationally. This is a crucial time when our sustained attention and participation are even more vital to overcome the climate crisis. This concludes today's climate briefing.
